***Trapper John, M.D.*** is an American [medical drama](/wiki/Medical_drama "Medical drama") television series and [spin-off](/wiki/Spin-off_(media) "Spin-off (media)") of the film *[M\*A\*S\*H](/wiki/M*A*S*H_(film) "M*A*S*H (film)")* (1970). [Pernell Roberts](/wiki/Pernell_Roberts "Pernell Roberts") portrayed the title character, a lovable surgeon who became a mentor and father figure in San Francisco, California. The show ran on [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") for seven seasons, from September 23, 1979, to September 4, 1986. Roberts played the character more than twice as long as had [Wayne Rogers](/wiki/Wayne_Rogers "Wayne Rogers") (1972–75) on the TV series *[M\*A\*S\*H](/wiki/M*A*S*H_(TV_series) "M*A*S*H (TV series)")*. The role of [Trapper John](/wiki/Trapper_John "Trapper John") was played by [Elliott Gould](/wiki/Elliott_Gould "Elliott Gould") in the film.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trapper_John,_M.D.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

*Trapper John, M.D.* focuses on Dr. ["Trapper" John McIntyre](/wiki/Trapper_John_McIntyre "Trapper John McIntyre") ([Pernell Roberts](/wiki/Pernell_Roberts "Pernell Roberts")) 28 years after his discharge from the 4077th [Mobile Army Surgical Hospital](/wiki/Mobile_army_surgical_hospital_(United_States) "Mobile army surgical hospital (United States)") (MASH) in the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"). During that time after the war, the character had mellowed considerably. He did not merely learn how to stop fighting the system but became a part of it, in a sense, as the Chief of Surgery at San Francisco Memorial Hospital. However, Trapper shows tremendous compassion toward his patients, often violating "established hospital procedures".

### Seasons 1–6

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trapper_John,_M.D.&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Seasons 1–6")]

Working with Trapper is an aspiring young professional named Dr. George Alonzo Gates ([Gregory Harrison](/wiki/Gregory_Harrison "Gregory Harrison")), usually referred to as Gonzo, who has a lot in common with Trapper, having also served in a MASH unit (albeit during the later [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War")). His sense of humor and love of life also reflect elements of Trapper's younger days. Gonzo resides in his [Winnebago](/wiki/Winnebago_Industries "Winnebago Industries") motor home (dubbed "The Titanic") in the hospital parking lot.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/e/e5/Trapper_John_MD_Cast.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trapper_John_MD_Cast.jpg)

The cast of *Trapper John, M.D.* season 6 (1984–85)

The show also involves several other characters that serve as hospital staff.

* Stanley Riverside II ([Charles Siebert](/wiki/Charles_Siebert "Charles Siebert")) is a pompous, status-seeking, but nonetheless capable doctor whose father is the head of the hospital board of directors. He later marries a dentist named E.J. ([Marcia Rodd](/wiki/Marcia_Rodd "Marcia Rodd"))
* Justin "Jackpot" Jackson ([Brian Stokes Mitchell](/wiki/Brian_Stokes_Mitchell "Brian Stokes Mitchell")) is a young doctor always interested in wagers.
* Gloria "Ripples" Brancusi ([Christopher Norris](/wiki/Christopher_Norris_(actress) "Christopher Norris (actress)")) is a young nurse who later adopts a sickly, homeless girl, Andrea. Her nickname Ripples was dropped after the first few episodes.
* Clara "Starch" Willoughby ([Mary McCarty](/wiki/Mary_McCarty_(actress) "Mary McCarty (actress)")) is an experienced nurse who had served in the Korean War with Trapper. McCarty died after the first season. In the show's continuity, at the beginning of season 2, her character is said to have married, retired, and moved away.
* Ernestine Shoop ([Madge Sinclair](/wiki/Madge_Sinclair "Madge Sinclair")) replaces Starch as the experienced older nurse from season 2 onwards. Sinclair picked up three Emmy nominations for her work as the dedicated and dignified Nurse Shoop.
* Arnold Slocum ([Simon Scott](/wiki/Simon_Scott_(actor) "Simon Scott (actor)")) is the hospital administrator who often clashes with Trapper and Gonzo, though there is strong mutual respect between all parties. Slocum—though charged with operating within regulations and keeping to a budget—clearly has sympathy and compassion for the patients. Scott suffered from Alzheimer's and made his final appearance in season six before retiring from acting.
* In season six, Trapper's son, J.T. McIntyre ([Timothy Busfield](/wiki/Timothy_Busfield "Timothy Busfield")), graduates from medical school and arrives at the hospital to work on his internship. He stays for the remainder of the run of the show.

### Season 7

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trapper_John,_M.D.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Season 7")]

The show underwent a number of changes during *Trapper John'*s seventh and final season.

* Christopher Norris left the series at the end of season six. Her character Gloria is replaced by new nurse Libby Kegler ([Lorna Luft](/wiki/Lorna_Luft "Lorna Luft")) at the start of season seven.
* Simon Scott, suffering from [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease"), had made his final appearance partway through season six. At the beginning of season seven, his character of hospital administrator Arnold Slocum is said to have retired. Slocum is replaced by administrator Catherine Hackett ([Janis Paige](/wiki/Janis_Paige "Janis Paige")).
* Added as a recurring player beginning with the season's third episode is ER service helicopter pilot and surgeon, Andy Pagano ([Beau Gravitte](/w/index.php?title=Beau_Gravitte&action=edit&redlink=1 "Beau Gravitte (page does not exist)")).
* Mid-way through the season, Gregory Harrison elected to leave the show. The character of Gonzo is written out, as he retires from medicine after having suffered a stroke. Gonzo is replaced by Jacob Christmas ([Kip Gilman](/wiki/Kip_Gilman "Kip Gilman")), a doctor who loses his wife in an accident, and is forced to become a single parent to his young son while adjusting to his new work environment.

Only nine further irregularly-scheduled episodes of *Trapper John* were produced after Harrison's departure.

After Harrison's last episode, the show was off the air for three weeks, then brought back on a different night (Tuesday) before being pre-empted three times in the next four weeks. A top 30 hit for most of its run when it aired on Sunday, *Trapper John, M.D.* fell out of the top 30 during season seven, and was canceled by season's end.

The final four episodes were aired on Thursday nights late in the summer of 1986, well after the show's cancellation had already been announced.

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trapper_John,_M.D.&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cast")]

### Main cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trapper_John,_M.D.&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Main cast")]

* [Pernell Roberts](/wiki/Pernell_Roberts "Pernell Roberts") as "Trapper" John McIntyre.
* [Gregory Harrison](/wiki/Gregory_Harrison "Gregory Harrison") as George Alonzo "Gonzo" Gates.
* [Mary McCarty](/wiki/Mary_McCarty_(actress) "Mary McCarty (actress)") as Clara "Starch" Willoughby (season 1)
* [Charles Siebert](/wiki/Charles_Siebert "Charles Siebert") as Stanley Riverside II.
* [Christopher Norris](/wiki/Christopher_Norris_(actress) "Christopher Norris (actress)") as Gloria "Ripples" Brancusi (seasons 1–6)
* [Brian Stokes Mitchell](/wiki/Brian_Stokes_Mitchell "Brian Stokes Mitchell") as Justin "Jackpot" Jackson.
* [Madge Sinclair](/wiki/Madge_Sinclair "Madge Sinclair") as Ernestine Shoop (seasons 2–7)
* [Timothy Busfield](/wiki/Timothy_Busfield "Timothy Busfield") as John "J.T." McIntyre Jr., M.D. (season 7; recurring season 6)
* [Lorna Luft](/wiki/Lorna_Luft "Lorna Luft") as Libby Kegler (season 7)
* [Kip Gilman](/wiki/Kip_Gilman "Kip Gilman") as Jacob Christmas (season 7)

### Recurring cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trapper_John,_M.D.&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Recurring cast")]

* [Simon Scott](/wiki/Simon_Scott_(actor) "Simon Scott (actor)") as Arnold Slocum, Hosp. Administrator (83 episodes, seasons 1-6)
* [Jessica Walter](/wiki/Jessica_Walter "Jessica Walter") as Melanie McIntyre, Trapper's ex-wife. (10 episodes, seasons 1–2, 4–7)
* [Richard Schaal](/wiki/Richard_Schaal "Richard Schaal") as Dr. David Sandler, who becomes Melanie's fiancé. (8 episodes, seasons 2–4, 6–7)
* [Janis Paige](/wiki/Janis_Paige "Janis Paige") as Catherine Hackett, the new administrator (15 episodes, season 7)
* Beau Gravitte as Dr. Andy Pagano, ER service helicopter pilot and surgeon. (10 episodes, season 7).
* Character actress [Lurene Tuttle](/wiki/Lurene_Tuttle "Lurene Tuttle") guest-starred six times in different roles.

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trapper_John,_M.D.&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Development")]

In a suit filed in New York state court, [Ingo Preminger](/wiki/Ingo_Preminger "Ingo Preminger"), producer of the 1970 motion picture *M\*A\*S\*H*, claimed that under his deal with 20th Century Fox, his production company had both the right of first refusal to produce any spin-off of the movie, and the right to fees from the use of the book and film's material. [New York State Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court "New York Supreme Court") Justice Martin Stecher found that Preminger's agreement with Fox did not give him the right to produce *Trapper John M.D.*, but did entitle him to a 25% share in profits from the show.[[1]](#cite_note-1) This decision was later cited by the same court in its 2008 decision in *Kellman v. Mosley*, involving a claim for royalties involving the [Easy Rawlins](/wiki/Easy_Rawlins "Easy Rawlins") detective series.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

It has sometimes been reported that the producers of the television series *M\*A\*S\*H* filed suit claiming they were entitled to royalties from the new show, arguing that it was based on the character as portrayed in their series by [Wayne Rogers](/wiki/Wayne_Rogers "Wayne Rogers"), but the producers of *Trapper John, M.D* successfully argued that it was based only on the earlier film and [Richard Hooker](/wiki/Richard_Hooker_(author) "Richard Hooker (author)")'s *[MASH: A Novel About Three Army Doctors](/wiki/MASH:_A_Novel_About_Three_Army_Doctors "MASH: A Novel About Three Army Doctors")*.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Both TV series were produced under deals with [20th Century Fox Television](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox_Television "20th Century Fox Television"), and the film was produced by parent company [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox").

The pilot includes photos and video clips (part of a dream) taken from the *M\*A\*S\*H* film and TV series, including a publicity photo of Trapper (as portrayed by Wayne Rogers) and Hawkeye ([Alan Alda](/wiki/Alan_Alda "Alan Alda")), and the character mentions Radar and Hawkeye by name, while waking up from a dream about his time at M\*A\*S\*H. However, none of the other M\*A\*S\*H characters appeared as characters, and references to Trapper's time in Korea were rare.
